coredata.dc:title: Novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-incorporated thin film composite (TFC) membranes for forward osmosis (FO) desalination
coredata.prism:issueName: Nanomaterials for Water Desalination: Recent Advances and Future Challenges
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shengjie
Li, Feng
Su, Baowei
Hu, Michael Z.
Gao, Xueli
Gao, Congjie
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports a novel class of thin film composite (TFC) membranes for forward osmosis (FO) desalination. The TFC membranes were fabricated via interfacial polymerization (IP) of aqueous mixture of polyethyleneimine (PEI) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with organic solution of trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on modified polyacrylonitrile (PAN) ultrafiltration substrates. The chemical structures and morphologies of the synthesized GQDs and the GQDs-incorporated membranes were studied by various characterization techniques. The synthesized GQDs exhibited a narrow size distribution of 1.0–4.0 nm with an average size of 2.19 nm, and the thickness were one to three graphene layers. The results showed that GQDs nanoparticles were covalently bonded to the polyamide chains. The optimized TFC membrane with 0.050 wt% GQDs loading exhibited quite hydrophilic and neutrally charged membrane surface, along with an enhanced water flux of 12.9 L m−2 h−1 and a comparable reverse salt flux of 1.41 g m−2 h−1 when DI water and 0.5 M MgCl2 were used as the feed solution and the draw solution, respectively. Also, the optimized GQDs-incorporated TFC membrane presented an especially good anti-fouling performance. Thus, incorporating the novel graphene nanomaterial into polymer membranes may present its great potential application in desalination, purification and wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin film composite
Forward osmosis
Graphene quantum dots
Interfacial polymerization
Polyamide
Membranes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical modification of the PAN substrates
Modeling flux and Dilutive internal concentration polarization
FTIR spectra of the TFC membranes
Contact angle and zeta potential of the TFC membranes
Intrinsic separation properties and the ICP of the TFC membranes
Effects of GQDs loading on the performance
Effect of draw solution concentration on the TFC performance
Anti-fouling performance of the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science foundation of China (No. 21476218 , 20976170 ), the Foundamental Research Fund for the Central Universities (No. 3003000841822012 ). This is MCTL Contribution No. 166. Dr. Michael Hu is partially sponsored by the US Department of Energy, Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Bioenergy Technologies Office program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The molecular structures of PEI (a) and TMC (b)
Diagram for the synthesis of GQDs.
The preparation process of the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes and the reaction mechanism scheme of PEI/GQDs and TMC.
FTIR spectra of the prepared GQDs.
(a) TEM image of the prepared GQDs and (b) the corresponding size distribution of GQDs.
(a) The AFM image of GQDs and (b) the corresponding height profile along the line.
FTIR spectra of the PAN, H-PAN, PEI/GQDs, TFC-0 and TFC-0.05 membrane.
The surface SEM images of the H-PAN substrate, the pristine TFC-0 membrane and the TFC-0.05 membrane.
The cross-sectional TEM images of the H-PAN, the pristine TFC-0 membrane, and the TFC-0.05 membrane.
The AFM images of the pristine TFC-0 membrane and the TFC-0.05 membrane
Contact angles of the PAN, H-PAN, H-PAN/PEI, TFC-0, PEI-GQDs, and TFC-0.05 membranes.
Zeta potential of the H-PAN, the pristine TFC-0 and the TFC-0.05 membranes.

Effects of GQDs loading on (a) water flux J V and reverse salt flux J S and (b) the ratio of reverse salt flux to water flux (J S/J V) (DI water and 0.5 M MgCl2 were used as feed solution and draw solution, respectively).

The effect of the draw solution concentration on the water flux and the reverse salt flux of the TFC-0.05 membrane (DI water and 0.5 M MgCl2 were used as feed solution and draw solution, respectively)
Forward osmosis fouling of the TFC-0.05 membrane in (a) AL-FS mode and (b) AL-DS mode
(100 mg L−1 HA or 100 mg L−1 BSA were used as the feed solution and 0.5 M MgCl2 were employed as draw solution, respectively).
Comparison of membrane characteristics of the GQDs-incorporated membranes in this study with literature data.
Intrinsic transport properties of the pristine TFC-0 and GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: DI water is used as the feed solution in the RO test with an applied pressure of 3 bar.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 500 ppm MgCl2 solution is used as the feed solution in the RO test with an applied pressure of 3 bar.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated based on the FO test under FO mode using 0.5 M MgCl2 as the draw solution and DI water as the feed solution.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the separation performance of the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes in this work with those FO membranes in the literature.

head.ce:title: Novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-incorporated thin film composite (TFC) membranes for forward osmosis (FO) desalination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology of Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao 266100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Correspondence to: B. Su, Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology of Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao 266100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports a novel class of thin film composite (TFC) membranes for forward osmosis (FO) desalination. The TFC membranes were fabricated via interfacial polymerization (IP) of aqueous mixture of polyethyleneimine (PEI) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with organic solution of trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on modified polyacrylonitrile (PAN) ultrafiltration substrates. The chemical structures and morphologies of the synthesized GQDs and the GQDs-incorporated membranes were studied by various characterization techniques. The synthesized GQDs exhibited a narrow size distribution of 1.0–4.0 nm with an average size of 2.19 nm, and the thickness were one to three graphene layers. The results showed that GQDs nanoparticles were covalently bonded to the polyamide chains. The optimized TFC membrane with 0.050 wt% GQDs loading exhibited quite hydrophilic and neutrally charged membrane surface, along with an enhanced water flux of 12.9 L m−2 h−1 and a comparable reverse salt flux of 1.41 g m−2 h−1 when DI water and 0.5 M MgCl2 were used as the feed solution and the draw solution, respectively. Also, the optimized GQDs-incorporated TFC membrane presented an especially good anti-fouling performance. Thus, incorporating the novel graphene nanomaterial into polymer membranes may present its great potential application in desalination, purification and wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Water scarcity has become a global concern with the trend of increasing world population, growing pollution, rising demands and declining quantity of drinking water [1]. In order to alleviate the global water crisis, extensive researches have been devoted to exploring economic and efficient technologies for desalination and water treatment. Although conventional technologies including multi-effect distillation (MED) [2], multistage flash evaporation (MSF) [3], electrodialysis (ED) [4], and reverse osmosis (RO) membrane processes [5] have been applied widely in desalination and have acquired many achievements, they are considered energy-intensive and relatively costly [6]. Alternatively, forward osmosis (FO), an osmotically driven membrane process that utilizes only natural osmotic pressure difference between the high and the low salinity streams across the semi-permeable FO membrane, has drawn widespread attention recently [7–10]. Compared to those pressure driven membrane processes such as RO, nanofiltration (NF), ultrafiltration (UF) and microfiltration (MF), the FO technology is a novel membrane technology for desalination and water treatment with advantages of low energy consumption, low membrane fouling [11,12], and high water recovery [13], etc. As a result, the FO process has been applied in various industries, including desalination [14,15], wastewater treatment [16,17], liquid food processing [18], pharmaceutical applications [6], power generation [9], and so on.

Generally, the main obstacle of FO process application is internal concentration polarization (ICP) [19], which is a significant phenomenon that includes dilutive ICP and concentrative ICP. The dilutive ICP occurs with the active layer facing the feed solution (i.e., AL-FS orientation) in which operation the draw solute is diluted around the support layer and the feed solute accumulates around the active layer. The concentrative ICP occurs with the active layer facing the draw solution (AL-DS orientation) in which operation the feed solute accumulates in the support layer. Both dilutive and concentrative ICP can reduce the effective osmotic pressure gradient for water transport and thus cause a severe decline in water flux [19]. In order to minimize the ICP and get desirable membrane performance with high water permeability, high salt rejection, and superior anti-fouling property, researchers have explored various FO membranes [20–27]. Thin film composite (TFC) membranes prepared by the formation of an active layer on the substrate via interfacial polymerization between an aqueous monomer and an organic monomer could be a good candidate among the various membrane types, since the substrate and the active layer could be optimized individually.

Recently, extensive efforts have been devoted to optimizing TFC membrane performances by using carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [28], carbon dots (CDs) [29], graphene oxide (GO) [30], graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) [31,32], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [33], etc. Incorporating GO or GOQDs into membranes has been fabricated which exhibited an enhanced water permeability about 50% to 200% and preferable antifouling property [30–32,34,35]. Shen et al. had reported a kind of TFC-FO membrane by incorporating GO nanosheets into MPD aqueous solution. The results showed that GO nanosheets reacted with MPD during ultrasonic treatment and were covalently bonded to the polyamide chains. The water permeability was enhanced about 60% and the antifouling propensity was improved of the GO-incorporated TFC-FO membranes compared with the pristine TFC-FO membrane [30]. Zhang et al. had prepared a thin film nanocomposite NF membrane by incorporating GOQDs into a tannic acid (TA) aqueous solution. The results showed that both TA and GOQDs reacted with isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) respectively during the IP process. The optimized GOQDs-incorporated NF membrane with polyurethane-polyamide active layer exhibited excellent antifouling property and preferable performance for the removal of organic dyes [32].

Researches found that smaller GO flakes provided extra shorter paths for water molecules to penetrate the freestanding GO membrane comparing with larger GO flakes [36], and membrane surface coated with the smaller GO exhibited higher antimicrobial activity than the membranes coated with the larger GO [37]. It was accepted that the size of GO had an important influence on the membrane characteristics and performance. GQDs possesses hydrophilic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the edge of the nanosheets, and hydrophobic aromatic ring on the plane of the nanosheets [38]. With even smaller particles size, which is typically below 20 nm [39], GQDs has much higher perimeter-to-surface ratio and provides more hydrophilic groups than GO and is more apt to combine with MPD to obtain a much firmer structure. Furthermore, GQDs can distribute in the skin layer more uniformly, the stretch of GQDs in the skin layer would be less, thus a smaller roughness and a much smoother surface can be achieved, which may be beneficial for the anti-fouling performance. The smaller GQDs particles size may even result in a thinner skin separation layer. All of these aspects would endow GQDs-incorporated FO membrane very good performances.

In this study, aiming at achieving excellent FO separation performance and enhancing its anti-fouling property, we prepared GQDs via pyrolyzing citric acid and fabricated novel TFC FO membranes via incorporating GQDs into an aqueous polyethylenimine (PEI) solution followed by interfacial polymerization with TMC. Recently, a similar research work has been reported for NF application with different GOQDs loadings [32], but none of the works has so far been attempted to incorporate GQDs in multi-amine PEI aqueous solution to prepare FO membranes by interfacial polymerization. In order to regularize the size of GQDs, for the first time, we have used UF-NF dual-membrane methods to purify the GQDs, and obtained average particles size below 20 nm. We studied the morphology and the fundamental property of the synthesized GQDs and the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes with detailed characterizations, and the effects of GQDs loading on the PA active layer and on the FO performance of the TFC membranes were studied as well.

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) UF membrane with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 50,000 Da was purchased from Suntar Membrane Technology (Xiamen, China) Co., Ltd. DL NF membrane with MWCO of 300 Da was purchased from GE Co. Ltd. 50% Polyethylenimine (PEI, number-average molecular weight of 1200 Da) aqueous solution, trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and humic acid (HA) were purchased from Aldrich. Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36%–38%), hexane, citric acid (99.5%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2) and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized (DI) water produced by a Flom water purification system (Qingdao, China) was used throughout the study. The molecular structures of PEI and TMC are shown in Fig. 1 .
GQDs was prepared by directly pyrolyzing citric acid according to the “bottom-up” approach [38,40]. The reaction mechanism is shown in Fig. 2 .

In brief, citric acid was subjected to thermal treatment at 200 °C, followed by a neutralization process and a dialysis process. 10 g citric acid was added into a 250 mL round bottomed flask and was heated to 200 °C for 30 min by a heating mantle. The obtained liquid was immediately added dropwise into 250 mL 1.0 M NaOH solution with vigorous magnetic stirring. The solution was further neutralized to pH = 7.0 by HCl. Subsequently, the solution was filtered by the PAN UF membrane with MWCO of 50,000 Da under 0.1 MPa to remove large particles. The resultant filtrate was collected and further filtrated by the DL NF membrane under 2.0 MPa. Diafiltration mode of NF operation was performed with periodically adding DI water whose amount equals to the NF permeate into the NF retentate till the conductivity of the NF permeate was less than 100 μs cm−1. The final NF retentate was the purified GQDs aqueous solution.

The synthesized GQDs was characterized by FTIR (magna-560, Thermo Nicolet, USA) with a resolution of 0.5 cm−1 in the wavenumber range of 4000–600 cm−1. The samples for the FTIR were dried in a 40 °C vacuum drying oven overnight before the measurement. The size and the surface morphology of the synthesized GQDs nanoparticles were analyzed by TEM (HT-7700, Hitachi, Japan) and AFM (Nano-3D, Nikon, Japan), separately. Before the measurement, the GQDs solution was first diluted to a reasonable content in order to avoid GQDs overlapping and cohering, and then TEM samples and AFM samples were prepared by dropping the GQDs solution individually onto the ultrathin carbon film and mica surface until the GQDs was completely dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical modification of the PAN substrates
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the surface hydrophilicity of the substrates, the PAN substrates were hydrolyzed by immersing into 2.0 M NaOH solution at 65 °C for 1 h according to our previous work [41]. The hydrolyzed PAN substrates were rinsed for several times with DI water to remove any remaining NaOH solution, and then were stored in DI water for subsequent usage. The as-prepared substrates were abbreviated as H-PAN. The pure water permeability of the H-PAN substrates was 65.5 LMH bar−1.
The GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes were prepared on the H-PAN substrates by interfacial polymerization (IP). The preparation process of the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes is shown in Fig. 3 .

The PEI/GQDs solution was served as the aqueous monomer solution in which the concentration of PEI polymer was fixed at 0.60 wt%, while the content (weight percent) of GQDs nanoparticles in the aqueous PEI polymer solution was kept as 0.025, 0.050, 0.075, 0.100 wt%, respectively. Then the dispersion of GQDs in PEI aqueous solution was taken by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min before the IP process.

First, the H-PAN substrates were immersed into the aqueous monomer solution for 15 min. After that, it was taken out and the remaining solution on the membrane surface was removed by draining vertically until no droplets was seen. Then the PEI/GQDs dip-coating membrane was dried in the air for an additional 1 min and was subsequently immersed in the organic monomer solution of 0.20 w/v% TMC/n-hexane for 1 min, which resulted in the formation of a polyamide (PA) active layer on the H-PAN substrates surface. Afterward, the prepared membrane was taken out and dried shortly in the air and then treated in a 70 °C oven for 10 min. Finally, the prepared TFC membranes were stored in DI water for further usage. The prepared GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes were labelled as TFC-0, TFC-0.025, TFC-0.05, TFC-0.075 and TFC-0.10, respectively, in which the numbers behind the “TFC-” mean the GQDs content in the aqueous monomer solution.

The characterization procedures of FTIR and AFM of the TFC membranes were similar to those of the GQDs. All the membranes samples were dried in the 40 °C vacuum drying oven overnight before the measurements. Surface morphology of the membranes was measured by scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The SEM samples were coated with gold before observation. The cross-section of the membrane samples was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, HT-7700, Hitachi, Japan). The TEM samples were frozen cut with resin imbedding before examination. The dynamic water contact angle of the membranes surface was measured by an automatic contact angle meter (DSA-100, KRÜSS, Germany) at room temperature. Each sample was measured at least five random points and then an average value was obtained. The zeta potential of the prepared membranes was measured by a self-made device and 0.001 M KCl electrolyte solution was chosen to measure it at room temperature. The zeta potential was obtained by calculating the Hemholtz-Smoluchowski equation [42].

The FO performances of the prepared membranes were carried out using a lab-scale cross-flow filtration unit with an effective membrane area of 28.26 cm2. DI water and 0.5 M MgCl2 were used as the feed solution and the draw solution, respectively. Both feed solution and draw solution were pumped with an average membrane surface flow velocity of 0.073 m s−1 according to the literatures [43,44]. A conductivity meter (DDSJ-308A, Rex, China) was used to determine the reverse salt flux of the draw solution. AL-FS orientation was chosen as the mode of FO experiments, i.e., the active layer side of the membrane is facing to the feed solution (DI water). Each test was pre-conditioned for 30 min to reach the steady state prior to the test. The curve of the water flux was drawn simultaneously by a computer data acquisition system via monitoring the mass variations of the feed solution. Each test was repeated for three times and then the average value was calculated.

The water flux (J v, L m−2 h−1) and the reverse salt flux (J s, g m−2 h−1) were calculated by the following Eq. (1) and Eq. (2).
(1)
J V
=
Δ V
A Δ t
(2)
J S
=
Δ
C S
V S
A Δ t
where ΔV is the permeate volume change which can convert from the mass variations of DI water, A is the effective membrane area, Δt is the measuring time interval, C t and V t are the salt concentration and the feed solution volume in the FO test, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modeling flux and Dilutive internal concentration polarization
ce:section.ce:para:
According to Loeb et al. [19] and Elimelech et al. [19,45], the flux behavior in the dilutive ICP for the FO process is expressed as Eq. (3).
(3)
K =
1
J w
ln
B + A
π
D , b
B +
J w
+ A
π
F , m
where K is the resistivity of the solute diffusion within the porous support layer, J w is the experimental permeate water flux, B is the salt permeability coefficient in the active layer, A is the pure water permeability coefficient, πD,b and πF,m are the osmotic pressure of the draw solution in the bulk and the osmotic pressure of the feed solution at the membrane surface, respectively. Ds is the salt diffusion coefficient. The parameters B, A, πD,b and πF,m for the membranes in this work were calculated according to the literature [19].

The fouling tests of the GQD-incorporated TFC-0.05 membrane were carried out under AL-FS and AL-DS mode, respectively, at room temperature. Three kinds of feed solutions (i.e. DI water, 100 mg L−1 HA aqueous solution, and 100 mg L−1 BSA aqueous solution) were used while 0.5 M MgCl2 was employed as the draw solution. Each anti-fouling test was performed for 8 h using one kind of feed solution. Subsequently, both the feed side and the draw solution side were cleaned by DI water for 30 min to eliminate any impurities on the membrane surface. For each feed solution, the fouling tests and cleaning cycles were carried out for three times. After that, the feed solution was changed for another anti-fouling test.

The successful preparation of GQDs was confirmed by FTIR, TEM and AFM characterization. As shown Fig. 4 , the characteristic peak at 3350 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups, carboxylate groups at 1645 cm−1 (CO bonds asymmetric stretching vibration) confirmed the existence of COOH functional groups on GQDs [40,46]. The bond at 1409 cm−1 was attributed to C–H, while the bond at 1569 cm−1 was assigned to C=C stretching vibration of aromatic ring, which demonstrated that the synthesized GQDs contained aromatic ring [47].

The size distribution of the synthesized GQDs nanoparticles analyzed by TEM was illustrated in Fig. 5 . The image showed well dispersed and 2D sheet morphology without apparent agglomeration. The size distribution of GQDs showed that the size of the synthesized GQDs was within the ranges of 1.0–4.0 nm and the average size was determined to be 2.19 nm.

The morphology of the GQDs was also observed by AFM and the result was shown in Fig. 6 . The thickness of the synthesized GQDs were within the rages of 0.5–2 nm, as shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b), suggesting that most of the GQDs particles have one to three graphene layers [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR spectra of the TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of the PAN, the H-PAN substrate, the H-PAN surface covered with PEI/GQDs, the pristine TFC-0 membrane, as well as the TFC-0.05 membrane were shown in Fig. 7 . The PAN membrane showed two characteristic peaks, 2243 cm−1 and 1732 cm−1, which were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the nitrile groups (–CN) and the carboxyl groups (–COOH), respectively. The characteristic peak at 1732 cm−1 was attributed to the fact that it was a copolymer of polyacrylonitrile and poly (methyl acrylate) [48]. For the H-PAN substrates, there still remained intensities of the nitrile groups after hydrolysis, suggesting that the hydrolysis of nitrile groups by alkali pretreatment was not complete and the nitrile groups inside the substrate was not hydrolyzed [41].

The pristine TFC-0 membrane exhibited distinct characteristic peaks of the amide groups at 1637 cm−1 and 1558 cm−1 [30,49]. However, the TFC-0.05 membrane exhibited characteristic peaks of the amide groups at 1649 cm−1 and 1543 cm−1 [30,49], Moreover, there is a slight enhancement of the peak at 1649 cm−1. The shift of the characteristic peaks of the amide groups clearly showed the influence of GQDs, since no other materials except GQDs was added to the TFC-0.05 membrane compared with that of the pristine TFC-0 membrane. The influence of GQDs can be further interpreted from the FTIR spectrum of the H-PAN surface covered with PEI/GQDs dip-coating layer, as also shown in Fig. 7. There exists a peak at 1670 cm−1 for the H-PAN surface covered with PEI/GQDs, as well as a peak at 1562 cm−1, both of which also belong to the characteristic peaks of amide groups [30,49]. The reaction mechanism between PEI and GQDs was quite similar to that between aniline and GQDs, since both PEI and aniline have amine groups that can react with the carboxyl acid groups of GQDs to form amide bonds [30,50]. This indicated that when GQDs was doped into the aqueous PEI solution under ultrasonic treatment, covalent amide groups linkage formed between PEI and GQDs. As the PEI/GQDs complex still has a lot of –NH2 groups, they could further conduct interfacial polymerization with the organic TMC solution and form amide covalent bonds during the IP process. Consequently, this could avoid the leaching of GQDs from the membrane active skin layer and improve the stability of the GQDs-incorporated membranes during the filtration process as well as the hydraulic cleaning process.

The surface SEM images of the H-PAN substrates, the pristine TFC-0 membrane and the TFC-0.05 membrane were shown in Fig. 8 . Some visible pores with mean diameter of 7–15 nm were observed on the surface of the H-PAN substrates. After interfacial polymerization reaction between PEI and TMC, a dense polyamide layer without obvious defects was formed. Both the pristine TFC-0 membrane and the TFC-0.05 membrane showed a ridge-valley structure on the active skin layer but the surface was relative flat, which was a typical morphology synthesized by IP between PEI and TMC [51,52]. Compared with the pristine TFC-0 membrane, the TFC-0.05 membrane showed a rougher surface than the pristine TFC membrane.

Fig. 9 showed the cross-sectional TEM images of the H-PAN, the pristine TFC-0 membrane, and the TFC-0.05 membrane. All these three membranes possessed structures of finger-like pores and thin skin layers. For substrate, the average thickness of the substrate layer is about 60 nm. For TFC membrane, there is no significant boundary between the substrate layer and the IP layer, the combination of the substrate layer and the IP layer of the pristine TFC-0 membrane is about 110 nm, and that of the TFC-0.05 membrane is about 105 nm. Therefore, there was no significant difference between the thickness of the IP layer of the pristine TFC-0 membrane and that of the TFC-0.05 membrane, both IP layers are about 50 nm thickness.

Fig. 10 showed the AFM images of the pristine TFC-0 membrane and the TFC-0.05 membrane. Clearly, the latter had a much rougher surface (Ra = 20.3 nm) than the former (Ra = 4.9 nm). The reason was that the GQDs presented in the aqueous monomer solution could react with PEI molecules by covalent linkages, so that the diffusion of PEI molecules would be retarded because of the enlarging PEI/GQDs structure and consequently larger grainy and convex morphology would form. It was believed that this active layer surface morphology would increase the water flux, since the greater surface roughness would result in the greater specific surface area of the membrane [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle and zeta potential of the TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The contact angles were shown in Fig. 11 . The contact angle of the unmodified PAN substrates was 50.7°. It decreased to 43.2° because of the hydrophilic carboxyl groups converted from −CN groups on the PAN substrates surface during the alkali pretreatment process [41]. The contact angle of the H-PAN/PEI membrane surface further reduced to 31.0° when the substrates surface was covered with more hydrophilic PEI molecules after being immersed in the aqueous solution. The contact angle of the pristine TFC-0 membrane was about 32.1°, slightly higher than that of the H-PAN/PEI membrane, since the hydrophilic groups (–NH2 and –NH–) of PEI molecules and acyl chloride groups of TMC molecules reacted to form amide groups and slightly reduced the hydrophilicity of PEI [54]. Compared with the H-PAN/PEI membrane, the PEI/GQDs membrane displayed a relative higher contact angle of 35.4°. It was attributed to the fact that GQDs nanoparticles have a relatively larger surface which mainly consisted of hydrophobic aromatic ring, while some of hydrophilic groups (–COOH and –OH) on the edges of the GQDs nanoparticles interacted with PEI molecules during the ultrasonic treatment (as shown in Fig. 3). Therefore, the surface of PEI/GQDs membrane exhibited a little less hydrophilic than that of the H-PAN/PEI surface because of the hydrophobic body of the GQDs. As a consequence, the GQDs-incorporated membranes (TFC-0.05) showed slightly less hydrophilic than the pristine TFC-0 membranes, and the contact angle of the TFC-0.05 membrane was slightly increased from 32.1° to 37.5°. However, with such a contact angle, the GQDs-incorporated membrane (TFC-0.05) was still quite hydrophilic [54].

The surface charge of the membranes was investigated through streaming potential measurements carried out over the pH range of 3.0–8.0. The results were illustrated in Fig. 12 . For the H-PAN substrates, the membrane surface was covered by carboxylate radical after alkali modification [41], which corresponded to the lowest isoelectric point (at about pH = 3). For the pristine TFC-0 membrane, the isoelectric point was at about pH = 6.3, indicating that the membrane surface was less charged than the H-PAN membrane surface. The TFC-0.05 membrane showed an even higher isoelectric point (close to pH = 7) owing to its less hydrophilic surface caused by the relatively hydrophobic GQDs incorporated, as can be seen in Fig. 11. This indicated that the density of the pendent acyl chloride would decrease a little after the IP reaction, and consequently, less carboxylic groups (–COOH) would form after the dissociation of acyl chloride. Therefore, the charge of the membrane surface was close to electric neutrality, which can be beneficial for the anti-fouling performance of the membrane [55].

The summary of the membrane structure parameters and the surface physic-chemical parameters in this work and those in the literatures is presented in Table 1 . It can be seen that the TFC-0.05 membrane in this work has much thinner active layer and smaller roughness, higher hydrophilicity, as well as more neutrally charged surface compared with other membranes in literatures. These parameters are beneficial for the separation and anti-fouling performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intrinsic separation properties and the ICP of the TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic transport properties including the water permeability coefficient (A), the salt permeability coefficient (B), and the salt rejection (R) for the pristine TFC membrane and the GQDs-incorporated membranes were provided in Table 2 . It was observed that the GQDs-incorporated membranes possessed higher water permeability than the pristine TFC-0 membrane, and the rejection of MgCl2 was also improved to some extent.

The effect of GQDs incorporation on the ICP of the prepared TFC membranes was analyzed by Eq. (3). It can be seen from Table 2 that the diffusion resistance K decreased gradually with the GQDs content increased up to 0.050 wt%, which indicated that at this GQDs content range, the incorporation of GQDs could promote the mass transfer inside the skin layer which was formed by PEI/GQDs and TMC, resulting in a smaller ICP extent. The promotion of the mass transfer by GQDs could be attributed to the ultra-thin thickness and the ultra-small size of GQDs nanoparticles which could provide many channels between the GQDs and the skin layer [58]. Therefore, the mass transfer resistance inside the membrane skin layer is decreased, i.e. the ICP extent is alleviated. It was observed that the extent of ICP can be mitigated by 21% with 0.050 wt% GQDs loading according to Table 2.

It is interesting to note from Table 2 that the incorporation of GQDs could help to increase the rejection to some extent. This suggests that GQDs could help to decrease the pore size of the substrate when PEI/GQDs was covered on the substrate surface, so that the average pore size of the substrate might be shifted to a smaller value. Therefore, the pores on the substrate surface would be more easily covered by the IP layer, and then more selective pores were formed. Similar rejection trends were found with GO incorporation and GOQDs incorporation in literatures [30,32].

However, with the further increase of the GQDs content, K increased gradually, indicating that the ICP extent became severe gradually. This might be due to the aggregation of GQDs at higher content. This aggregation might result in more interstitial gaps or voids in the PA layer during the IP layer fabrication process, resulting in more easily penetration of the salt ions because of those extra channels [29]. Therefore, the 0.050 wt% GQDs loading is preferred from the viewpoint of lower ICP extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of GQDs loading on the performance
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of GQDs loading on the water flux and the reverse salt flux of the prepared TFC membranes were evaluated at AL-FS mode using DI water and 0.5 M MgCl2 as the feed solution and the draw solution, respectively. The results were shown in Fig. 13 .

It was shown that all the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes obtained a higher water flux and a lower reverse salt flux than the pristine TFC-0 membrane. At 0.050 wt% GQDs loading, the TFC membrane showed the best separation performance, with the highest flux, 12.9 L m−2 h−1 (Fig. 13 (a)), and the lowest J S/J V, (Fig. 13 (b)), the water flux was remarkably enhanced by 22.0% and the reverse salt flux decreased by 32.2% than the pristine TFC-0 membrane. The increase of the water permeability could be mainly due to the water channels formed between the GQDs nanoparticles and the IP active layer, which provided extra channels for the water penetrate [58]. Moreover, additional driving force originated from the hydrogen bonding interactions between the unreacted carboxyl/hydroxyl groups of GQDs and water molecules would facilitate water molecules to penetrate within the water channels. Further, the much rougher surface of the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes were also beneficial for the water flux enhancement, since the effective contact area between the water molecules and the membrane was expanded [53].

However, the water flux decreased at even higher GQDs loading. This can be attributed to the agglomeration of GQDs which would not provide so many nano-corridors, and even might lead to the blocking of pores on the surface of the substrate; excessive GQDs nanoparticles at higher loading could also lead to much tortuous and elongated transfer pathways for water molecules to transport within the active layer [54]. Therefore, the hydraulic resistance increased, and the water flux decreased.

As Fig. 13 (b) showed, with the increase of GQDs loading, the rate of reverse salt flux to water flux (J S/J V) decreased at first, and it reached the lowest at 0.050 wt% GQDs loading, then increased slightly, which corresponded to the reverse trend of the solute rejection of the TFC membranes. Since the TFC-0.05 membrane showed the highest water flux and relatively low reverse salt flux, this membrane was selected for further anti-fouling tests.

Table 3 showed the comparison of the separation performance between TFC membranes prepared in this work and those reported in literatures [28,59–67]. The TFC-0.05 in this work has relative low Js and comparable Jv when DI water and 0.5 M MgCl2 were used as the feed solution and the draw solution, respectively, while Js/Jv is relative low among those of the literatures, which means lower reverse diffusion of MgCl2 in our work [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of draw solution concentration on the TFC performance
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the draw solution concentration on the FO performance of the TFC-0.05 membrane was presented in Fig. 14 . Both the water flux and the reverse salt flux increased with the increase of the draw solution concentration. However, the increasing rate of the flux quickly slowed down at concentration of the draw solution higher than 0.5 M. Therefore, the MgCl2 draw solution of 0.5 M was chosen for the further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-fouling performance of the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the anti-fouling performance of GQDs-incorporated membrane in different mode (AL-FS mode and AL-DS mode), the anti-fouling tests of the TFC-0.05 membrane were taken and the results were shown in Fig. 15 . Both HA and BSA were used as model foulants according to literatures [28,68].

It was found that the TFC-0.05 membrane showed an especially good anti-fouling property in both AL-FS mode and AL-DS mode operation. After 25 h′ testing, there was only quite slight flux decline under the AL-FS mode operation by using either HA or BSA as model organic foulant. The flux almost recovered to the initial water flux after being cleaned by DI water for 30 min. This meant that the fouling of the TFC-0.05 membrane was reversible in the AL-FS mode operation. Therefore, the novel GQDs-incorporated membranes are promising in FO application.

Compared with the AL-FS mode operation, the flux under the AL-DS orientation decreased greatly, with a sharp drop to about 59% for HA and 68% for BSA. The reason was that the foulant in the feed solution can easily enter the porous substrate layer and accumulate under the AL-DS mode operation [28].

In this study, novel GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes for FO applications were fabricated via interfacial polymerization between aqueous PEI/GQDs solution and organic TMC solution on the H-PAN substrate. The synthesized GQDs has a 2D sheet morphology with an average size of 2.19 nm and a thickness of one to three graphene layers. Covalent bonds were formed between GQDs and PEI, thus improving the stability of the GQDs-incorporated membranes during the filtration process as well as the hydraulic cleaning process. The GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes have a thin active layer about 50 nm, a surface roughness of about 20 nm, a quite hydrophilic surface with a contact angle of 37.5°, and a neutrally charged membrane surface. The FO separation performance of the TFC membranes was enhanced with reasonable loading of GQDs. The fouling of the GQDs-incorporated TFC membranes was almost reversible in the AL-FS orientation, indicating a good prospect for FO applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science foundation of China (No. 21476218, 20976170), the Foundamental Research Fund for the Central Universities (No. 3003000841822012). This is MCTL Contribution No. 166. Dr. Michael Hu is partially sponsored by the US Department of Energy, Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Bioenergy Technologies Office program.
